speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This second conversation with Kate Sanderson Waldron took place at her home in Bovill on August 25, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager. It was like in your family. How your parents raised you. This is something we like to ask people because it's so different it seems nowadays than it

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: used to be. Well, my mother was a devout Christian and my brother John was converted when he did he ever tell you about his conversion?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Actually I didn't ask him about that but you talked to me about it last time I was here.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: And he was a Christian and he's the oldest one in the family. So my father was working as a timber cruiser. I guess it was for the Potlatch. And of course he wasn't home too much at the time. He was home at times but he was gone most of the time. So it was Mama and my brother John that was our example you see. We had a Christian home. We were raised in a Christian home. We went to church and Sunday school every Sunday. And when Dad was home, why? My dad was just like buyers. He just loves to cook. And he was a good cook. And he'd always have Sunday dinner and we could always ask company home for Sunday dinner because Mama knew that Dad would have a good dinner going. My dad was an awfully nice man. He was raised a southern gentleman. His mother died in the Civil War. She had a baby and his baby was only three weeks old and they were crossing a river in the east there. I don't know, I don't remember where it was. But in the Civil War and the bridge was burning at the time they crossed and it went down with them. And my aunt, or my, you can see she would be my grandmother. My grandmother was nearly drowned. She took pneumonia. Her baby was only three weeks old and they saved the baby but my grandmother died. That was my father's mother. And he was taken by an aunt and raised in the south. And he loved his dear old Negro manny. He just loved her. He said, I couldn't have had a finer mother than that Negro woman was. And we were raised to respect the Negro people. There was no class hatred in our family because my dad loved the Negro people because he was raised by such a fine Negro woman. That is, she was the nurse in the aunt that raised him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he Swedish? Was he Swedish? Was he Swedish? I had thought that he came from Swedish people.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: The name indicates that but that's not so. My grandfather, his father was a full-blooded Englishman and his mother was a full-blooded French woman. She was a French girl. And during school years there was quite an argument. Every time I was in high school we had a teacher and they'd think that we were Swedish people but we were not. I can't go ahead and explain why but my dad did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What part of the south then did he grow up in?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, just down south. I don't know. I don't even remember. No, I remember that because.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know the Wells family at all out here? You know the Negro family that lived in Deary?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well I knew of them. I think Byers and John knew them. They were up here long before I was. John especially. Byers wasn't. He came when I did because he was just a boy. He's six years younger than I am. But I remember the Wells, yes. I waited on the ladies when I was working in my store. I waited on those Negro ladies. They were nice women. At least nice to wait on. That's all I knew about them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well you know, speaking about your parents, what I was wondering about is one thing. Were they very strict with you or would you say lenient?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Did they what?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they very strict with you or would you say lenient the way they raised you?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: The way we were raised? Yes. Yes, indeed my mother was a strict woman. We were taught obedience. And I'll tell you if we got sassy now, we were slapped down right now. Mother wouldn't put up with that at all. But we were taught right and wrong. And we were taught manners, good manners. Mother was particular about that. And we had a very happy home. We were, I think, exceptionally happy home. I never quarreled with my brother, John, in my life. And he's 92 years old, 1986. And he has never said an unkind word to me in his life. Now that's something. And I've lived right around him too. But it's as though we were separated because we lived. And he was a marvelous brother. He was the oldest one of the family. And he was a wonderful brother. In those days, we burned wood stoves. And we had a cellar. And you had to go outdoors and go clear around the house, down into the cellar to get the vegetables. And if we girls would ask him, it was only us two girls and the rest were boys, why? He never once ever refused to go. If we needed a pail of water, he got it. If we needed some wood for the stove, he got it. If my sister was making a cake and she needed sugar, she'd ask him and he'd go clear downtown and get whatever she needed. He was a good man, a good boy. Of course, Byers was the baby in the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now it sounds like, that sounds unusual.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, it was unusual. We had a nice family. We were a very close family. After we were all married, and dad was up here working, why we all come home, lived up in here. That's why we're all up in here, to be near home. And every Sunday, my sister and I would make the cakes and the salads or the pies, whatever we're gonna have. Mama would take care of the meat and the vegetables. And we would all be at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The whole family?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: The whole family and the grandchildren. So that was quite rare too. Byers wasn't married at that time. And John wasn't yet married. But after they were married, why it was the same thing, it was their family's then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, tell me what you think about what it was that made your family a close one.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, I think it was our raising, our teaching. And my brother John was a Christian, a devout Christian. And my mother was a Christian. And we associated with the best of people, the church people. And the doctors and his wife were great friends of my mothers and fathers. They were nice people. They didn't go to the same church that we did, but they were, my father and Doc Dart were the greatest of friends. And Doc Dart was our doctor. And we were just raised and educated and poops. But we were a nice family. We were a close family. And I know that's why my husband wanted to come back here after his health broke and he was sick. He wanted to come back to Bogle. And I know now it was because he wanted to leave me close to my family. I know that's the reason because we were living in Moscow and had been there for about 12 years, 10, 12 years. And he wanted to come back home. So we owned this home. And we were only here two years when he was taken sick. And then we weren't old enough for our social security. So I went to work. We moved to Moscow and I went to work in Moscow. I clerked for years. And then when we got old enough to draw our social security, why we came back here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first meet him? When did you first meet him? Maybe I should sit over here. Maybe that'd be better so you can hear me better.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yes, I can. My hearing isn't too good at times. And it isn't real good today. I often wonder if the altitude affects it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the weather, the humidity.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yeah, it affected.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first meet Mr. Waldron?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I met him the 29th of March. On an Easter Sunday. And we went walking. We didn't have cars in those days. And there'd be a group of us girls that'd get together and we'd go out walking. And generally, that early in the spring, we'd just walk up the railroad track for a nice walk. And there was three of us and we were walking up the track. And Ray was just from Minnesota. He'd come out here to be with his brother. Their mother had died. And he came out here to be with his older brother and he was working in the sawmill there in Palouse. And then another man, another young man fellow. And they'd come walking up the track behind us. Well, the girls that were with me, the two girls, first one to turn around and look back. And the boys were laughing and saying, pretty soon the other one turned around and looked back. And then they said, well, now just wait. The third girl looked, turned around and looked back. And I was the third girl. And Ray, my husband, he says, I bet you a dollar that that girl will never turn around. And they bet with him and I didn't turn around. Ha ha ha ha. Well, they finally caught up with us. And Jay introduced me to his brother Ray. And Ray was from the East. And I had three, four girlfriends. Ray had been in town a couple of weeks. And the girls, a lot of them had met him. And I was at her home. And there was a group up there. And they said, we've got one boy that you're not gonna meet in this kitty. They called me Kitty. My name was Catherine and they called me Kate and Kitty. And, but the girls called me Kitty. And I said, well, who's that? And they said, it's Jay's brother. And we're gonna make sure you don't meet him. And I said, well, I don't wanna meet him. I said, I'm not interested in him anyway. I said, I don't care. And I had a steady fellow. But my steady fellow had taken off for Texas. He was from Texas and he was gone. At the time I met Ray. And so when Jay asked, I was the girl that Jay wanted his brother to go with. And that's why he wanted me to meet. He wanted me to meet his brother. That's why they were following us up the track. I learned this all later. I didn't know it, of course, at the time. But I was the girl that Jay wanted his brother to meet. And I knew Jay. And so I met Ray. Well, it didn't take me long. When I got with those girls, I said, would you girls think you're so smart? But I'll tell you, I met that kid. And they said, where'd you meet him? He said, we didn't introduce you. And I said, you didn't have to. I said, I met him last Sunday. I said, his brother Jay introduced me to him. And Ray and I went together. And my boyfriend came back from Texas. But I liked Ray much the best and I went with Ray.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you started dating?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I was 16.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do when you, to go out together in those days? What was the dating like?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No, there wasn't really, there are many places to go. We just meet together in different homes, a group of us. That's the way, really, we spent our time. And then we'd go walking together, or out in a horse and buggy, riding. My brother John, that was another thing about my brother John. He'd be going with some girl. And he always liked to have Ray and I with him, or the boys that I was going with. It didn't make any difference. It made four of us see it was much nicer. And John was so much older, but he went with younger girls. It'd be girls along about my age. And that way why we were, four of us were always together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Ray much older than you? Right?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh no, he was only six months. His birthday was in March. Nine was in August.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he'd come out as a very young fellow.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yes, he was just a boy. He quit high school, a little mutt. And he came out west to be with his brother. His mother was gone, you see. And he got Jay, who sent for him, to give him money. Jay was quite a bit older than Ray. Jay was one of the oldest children of the family, and Ray was next to the younger. And he went to work in the sawmill there. He tied bundles, boarded at a fine home there. A big man of the mill, Maxwell, was his name. And he and his brother boarded with him. And then Ray and Jay were great friends of the big, taut-latch man, the name of Kendall. And they had a boy that was a great friend of Jay's. They grew up together. Those kids all grew up together, the Kendall kids. And Mr. and Mrs. Kendall and Ray's father and mother were great friends. And of course, when the boys came out west, here's why the Kendall just put them right into jobs, you see. It was a big pull for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was their position with Potlatch? Do you remember?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No, because he was one of the head men. And Frank became, his son became a big head of the, one of the big men. No, they were situated in Spokane. They were in Palouse for a long time, but they finally were in Spokane. But they were big men of the, of the, taut-latch for years until they died. I have a deck of cards here that, the largest white pine tree in the world was cut here in Bovill. And Frank Kendall sent that to my husband when that tree was cut down. And you know, I showed that to somebody. I haven't any idea who. And I had the card that was in it that told her the feet of lumber and when it was cut and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, that's, that's beautiful. I really like that.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: And I have a piece of wood that my, that the day they cut that tree, that my husband got there. And this is a piece of the wood from the tree. I don't know whether you can read the writing on it or not. It's getting so old. It's 65 or 66 years old, you see. You might if I, if I could use this little magnifying glass that I have to use. So it's quite a keepsake. But I, I just give my eye teeth to know who stole my card that was in here. I've got a few other cards in here that I got from the Wanigan. You know what a Wanigan is?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: A river, bowcocks. Yes, yes. And it's a picture of the, I had an eight to ten picture of it. But my nephew, Chet Morris, knew the men that were in the logging on that river, the last trip that they made. And he didn't get one. And he knew those men and had worked with them. And I gave him that picture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it about him that made you like him say so much better than this fellow from Texas? What was it?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, I like the fellow from Texas. He was a dandy, nice kid. He was, I often think about Tip. We called him Tip. And his mother died on the trip from Texas. His father come up and got work up in this country. And he brought his mother and his two little sisters from Texas. And his mother died on the trip. She took sick and died just instantly, a heart attack or something like that. And he was only 17 then. And he brought his mother and his little sisters. And he went to work there in Plews. And was just a number one kid. He was a nice boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it about Ray that was so special?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Ray was kind of, he'd been raised differently. I could tell that, even though I was just a girl. His father was a traveling man. He traveled for the McCormick Company. I don't know whether that company still is today or not, but it's a big company. And he was a very attractive man. And he used to take first one boy and then the other with him a lot, traveling. And Ray had beautiful manners. He was polished. That travel, you know, gives him a polish that us ordinary kids don't get, because we don't travel around. And I think that's what fascinated me. And then he had a nice personality. And he was sweet and kind. He was a nice sweetheart. And very polite and thoughtful. He's kind of a fellow that a girl would like. Women always did like him. Even after I was married, women liked my husband.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you got married then?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, 18. I would say 18. Ray was only 19.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your parents?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: We were married and went and lived with his father down in southern Idaho. My children were born in southern Idaho. And his father then came out west and became a real estate man. And we lived there. And Dad had 168 acres of land down there in southern Idaho in Parma. And we were down there until we came to Bogle when my baby was, my little girl was six months old. But work just wasn't plentiful down there in southern Idaho. And I knew that my father held a position here, that if Ray and I come up here, my dad would see that Ray would get a good job, you know. So we came up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At that time, at that age, did you feel then that you were well prepared to raise a family?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, I guess so. I dearly loved my youngsters. And when my little girl was three years old, I started a Sunday school of my own. I financed it. And we lived in what's called the old slab town. And there was a number of families there. They even had a school out there. There were so many children. And I had about 14, and I had little Catholic children. I had lots of little Catholic children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you know what I mean. I'm thinking of the way, well, you know, nowadays, people seem to, a lot of young people feel they need to have a whole lot of resources, you know.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To start a married life.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I didn't feel that way. What I had was nice, but we left all of our furniture down in southern Idaho. And I lived in a little two-room shack up there at Camp 8, just like all the other women did, you know. We just had little houses to live in. I had one great big room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I didn't realize that the wives were living right at the camp, along with the men. But they were.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: You see, that was the main shop that my dad had there. And at one time, he had 700 men under him there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At Camp 8?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Uh-huh, at Camp 8. And John's father-in-law was farming at that camp, Camp 8, for a long time, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many married men at the camp, many women living there?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yes, we had. It was not so, children were not so, they had a school out at Camp 8.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Slabtown and Camp 8 pretty much the same, or was Slabtown in a different place? Was Camp 8 right at Slabtown?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, yes, it was about like walking downtown here. We were that close. I was trying in my mind to count the houses. But there were quite a number of houses because dad had a house out there, too. Dad had quite a big house. We lived out there for, oh, for two, three years. Then when the forest fire came, that was in 1914, why, after the forest fire came, why, I moved downtown here. I was the first woman in this town that had hot and cold water. Oh, really? Uh-huh. And oh, boy, was that something. I was so happy that I could, the people moved away and wanted to rent their house, that owned the house. And I rented it. And I don't remember what rent I paid, but it was big rent for Dan in those days. And my brother Frank, he's dead now. He just had a fit. He just thought I was crazy to pay that much rent. But it wasn't very much. It's 15, 20 dollars. I don't know what it was, I don't remember. But I remember how Frank thought, of course, Frank's younger than I am. And he was married. And I don't know whether he wanted the house and was mad because I got it or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that house have hot and cold running water in it at the time? Uh-huh, yeah.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: At the time, that's why I rented it, was because it had hot and cold water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were out at Camp 8, I can't imagine there was too, or maybe there was. Was there much of a social life?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, yes, yes. We came downtown to everything. To Bovill or something? Uh-huh. We had to train. We could get on a train and come down here and do our shopping. And oh, about, what time was it? It did give us two, three hours downtown here. And then at four o'clock, it went back. And that gave us time to get home to get dinner and supper, you know. And we belonged to everything down here. All the clubs that they had, we belonged to all the dancers. And things like that, shows and everything. We came down. We didn't have any cars, but we got down here. Even if we had to walk. It wasn't too far. I walked it many, many times. But we had social life. It's always been us. It is now. I was off to a big party yesterday. And my lady friend that was there in that trailer up there, she had a beautiful party. She had a lot of people from Deary. And there's lots going on here all the time. The kids go to Moscow and bowl. They have two different bowling leagues. And they play tennis downtown. Even the postmistress plays tennis down there. And they have their church activities. There's two classes of people here. There's the tavern class. Then there's the church class of people. There's two distinct classes. But they're not up-at-your-hatful towards each other. But there's a lot of them that never darken a church door. And there's a lot of us that do. But we are not enemies by any means.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the same way back in the teens?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yes. It was the same way. It's always been the same way. But they have bridge clubs and pineapple clubs. And forget me not clubs. At Christmas time, I'm usually invited to... all kinds of parties. All the time. Except the card parties. I don't play cards. Of course they fill the tables with their friends that play cards. But the other clubs, I'm always invited.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there people in Bovill that belong to both groups? Or does it have to be one or the other? Either tavern or church.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, I suppose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was just wondering.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I go to church in Moscow. Every Sunday we go to church in Moscow. But when I can't go, I go over here and I go to Lady's Aid over here all the time. I'm the oldest member of the Lady's Aid, because I've been in that aid for 64, 65 years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the Lady's Aid do when you were first in it?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, on Mondays the first month, we call it work day. And now, they so... We used to give big dinners. But the older women have all moved away. And the younger women just won't take it up. We used to give smokest bars. And people came from all over the country. We really made big money because the cooks were so good and they put on a smorgasbord. And we have a beautiful recreation room here downtown that the Potlatch made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the smorgasbords, that would be the first Monday, or would that be a special occasion?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: The first Monday is work day. Right now, the aid is making lap robes for the homes in Moscow, for the nursing homes, for the old people. They are chilly and they like lap robes. And that's a project that we're on now. And they give food sales. She's part of the program, and she gives some wonderful lessons on religion, on the Bible. She's very capable and very able. She's really the finest they've ever had here, I think. And I've been here a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was thinking about in the early years, in the teens, what the Lady's Aid was like at that time.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, back at that time it was just like that. We had more food sales and more big dinners. The Chamber of Commerce from different places would come in and hire the aid to put on a big dinner. The Eastern Star would meet here and they would put on a big dinner. We would put it on for them. We made good money all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the Lady's Aid would put on the dinner for the Eastern Star?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: For these people, you know. They'd want us to because they were such good cooks. We had a nice recreation room. Bogle was quite famous. People liked to come to Bogle. They enjoyed coming. It was up in the woods and, you know, it was quite an outing for them. They would come in so that they could come in. They used to have big dances. People would come clear as far as Garfield. To Bogle? To Bogle for the dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would the proceeds from these dinners go for? Would it go for a church, keeping up the church and that sort of thing?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: It went to the Lady's Aid to keep the church going. And it does now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Red Cross work?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, we had the Red Cross. I worked on the cancer drive for twelve years. And the cancer drive and the heart drive, they still keep that up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was thinking during the First World War when they had a lot of Red Cross activity. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: We made lots of knits. We worked very busily in the aid for the Red Cross. It helped that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Mrs. T.P. Jones?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, my. Do I remember my land? Yes. She was just like a mother to me. Was she? Oh, yes. Yes, I thought lots of T.P. They used to take me. Of course, I was very young at that time. But after I was up here and lived here, my sister and my sister were sick. And in the hospital there for so long in Moscow, Mrs. T.P. kept her room just like a bar. Beautiful flowers. Not just one bouquet, but the room was just filled with beautiful flowers. My father and T.P. were the greatest of friends. Greatest of friends. And Mrs. T.P., she liked my sister and I very much. She liked our whole family. She liked my mother very much, too, and my father. And she was very, very nice to us. And so were the salesmen that would come up. Of course, that was to their advantage, I suppose. But they had one salesman in particular that thought so much of my dad. Of course, dad would give him good orders, you know. That's true. But my, he just showered us with gifts. I used to, I let my daughter have it. I used to have a cut glass decanter about that high. It was the most beautiful thing. He even gave my sister-in-law a little diamond ring on our birthday. And he gave my mother just, oh, everything that was nice. She just had everything that he thought that she might enjoy. He was so good to our family. Thought the world of my mother and father.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As a salesman, what did he sell?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, it would be things for the shop. It would be things for the shop up there. Whatever they needed, they'd order through him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were telling me before about working as a funky. I was wondering, prior to that, to your doing that work, did you ever do work for pay, or was it just the family work that you did?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No. The only work I ever did. I went to business school. I went to business school in Spokane, but my sister was sick. I went for six months, and mother wrote and told me that I'd have to come home and help take care of my sister. She had two little girls. Mama said that she just cries all the time because she wants you home. She wants to see you. I used to go down every day and see her and visit with her. I had to leave business school and come home and help take care of my sister till she died. I helped take care of those girls even at that because Leila would send me money, and I bought all her clothes in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did your sister die from?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: She died of TB. That's what the doctor said, but I say she died of cancer. I'll tell you why I say that. Dr. Gibson was a wonderful doctor. He's the doctor that we had. He ran the hospital here for a long time. He had another doctor come up with him, and they were examining my sister. I was in the room. When that other doctor was examining my sister, he said to Dr. Gibson, there was a lump, a big lump. They found it inside of her here somewhere. This doctor said to Gibson, he says, well, what is that big lump? I heard him say that. Dr. Gibson says, that's why I had you come up here. You tell me. I don't think she ever died of TB. I think she died of cancer. I worked for 12 years on the cancer drive. Every spring they have us come to Moscow, and doctors show us pictures and talk to us about cancer. I learned a lot, and I'm positive she had cancer, but the doctor didn't know it. In those days, cancer wasn't hardly known at all in those days. I never heard of cancer at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your sister, how did she...

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Her husband was head carpenter up here. They came up here when their baby was born. He was a first-class carpenter. The rest of his life, till he retired, he was head carpenter here at the Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It must have been awfully sad for her too, to be so sick with her children.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Mom and I had quite a time. It was her girl. I just got my arm out of the cast a couple of weeks ago. I fell and broke my arm in May. I saw that. It was three months yesterday. It was her girl that took care of me. She just took beautiful care of me. That's Mrs. Chet Morris. She was my baby. When my sister died, she gave me both girls. She says, I want you to have both girls. Their father paid all their expenses. In fact, he boarded with me for a long, long time, till he married again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you raised the girl?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: But I raised the girls, yes. I love them just as dearly as if... They're lots closer than just nieces. They're just lots closer. I just adore both of them. She just gave me beautiful care. I wouldn't stay down to her house because I wanted to be home. I wanted my own bed. I knew it would make it much easier for her if I was here because I got up all the time and got my own breakfast. There was one time I couldn't. I got up and my ankle was so swell that I couldn't even walk. I had to send John when he come to see how it was. I had to send him over to Brady's at 9 o'clock to come over and get my breakfast. But otherwise, I got my own breakfast all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were the girls when their mother died?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, one was 13 and one was about 15.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they take it at the time? Was it difficult for them to adjust to them?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, no. They had been with me so much all their lives. They dearly loved me. I was really more of a mother to them and their own little mother because she was bed fast for years. We had her clear to male brothers. She was bed fast for years. She had knee trouble after Virginia. That's the one that took care of me. After that baby. She never should have had that baby. She had that knee trouble. We sent her clear to male brothers. But they just kept her for a month or two. My dad went back to see how she was. The doctors told her, said, you've got to take that girl home with you. She's so homesick that we can't do a thing for her. They put a weight on her foot. She was in bed for four or five years. She got so she could be up and around and keep her house. But she had hired help all the time. Had to have a hired girl. I helped her an awful lot. We took care of the little girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When she was bedridden, was she at her home or at your mother's? She was. When she was bedridden, was she at her own home?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, yes. She was in her own home and had hired a girl. But Mama was up there. I don't know how often, but two, three, every couple of days to see that everything. I lived up close to her. I was there every day to see how she was. I used to go over every afternoon, sit on her bed, and talk and visit with her. Take care of the kids. Look after them. They need the paddling I gave them. Then they bought a house downtown here. One of the nice houses downtown. It's burnt down now. It's gone many years. They bought that. I lived there. After Sis died, I moved right in. Urban lived with me then, her husband, until he married again. He married a school teacher here. Then the oldest girl got married. I had the other girl until she went to college. Then she married. She's always lived right here. The other girl lives down in Clarkston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his last name? Her name. Your brother-in-law's. Fisher.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I.O. Fisher. His wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Marie Fisher.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yes, it's Marie Fisher. That was his second wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were in Spokane, then what was your idea? What did you have in mind?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I was going to become a teacher. I went to night school for three years while I was in Spokane. I just reviewed. My idea was then I was going to become a teacher. After I had to quit business school and come home and help take care of Leila, I just gave up. I didn't ever try then to go on because the depression came along. My husband went over on the Clearwater and was going to get rich digging gold. I'll show you how much gold he got. I don't know what it's worth today, but he was over there all summer. He and another fellow. They worked like whiteheads. Here's the gold. At the time that he got it, it was a dollar and eighty cents worth, but I don't know what it would be now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a summer's work, huh?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: He looked like a million dollars when he came home. It was good for him. I went down to Troy with my daughter. I worked in the store down at Troy for a while during the depression. My husband went into the post office. When work opened up again up here, he came back up here and we moved to Moscow. He didn't want to live in Bovill. He thought it would be better if we lived in Moscow. I went to work in Moscow. Then when we became 65, we could get our Social Security while we moved back to Bovill. We had his home here. We made up our mind. My husband's health broke. We were only here two years when his health broke completely.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were in Spokane going to school there for six months, did you have to work your way through?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No. My husband was working and was making good money. My mother and dad helped me some. Of course, I expected to pay and did pay it all back to them. I paid back every cent of it. It ended my career really as working. I did really want to become a teacher. That's what I wanted to become. It just didn't pan out. I became a teacher. I was a teacher. I was a Sunday school teacher. I didn't teach little children at all. I never had that privilege. I love little kids. I taught young married people and college students and a few high school pupils. They had enough high school pupils to make a class. I had college students and young married people. I enjoyed it because I had to study like a whitehead to teach a class like that because they could just throw questions at you all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: True. They'd be challenging.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yes. It was challenging. I studied, believe me. I was the one that really learned by doing it. I never regretted it because I really enjoyed it very, very much. It was a hobby with me. I loved the class. I loved it. I do now. I love our young people there in Moscow. This little silver dish that I have, I was on the church board 25 years when they presented that to me. I was on the church board for about 30 years. Then I went to a big camp meeting and they had an instructor from California and he was the best speaker. He said, if there's any of you people in this great audience, there's hundreds of people there, and you've been on the church board for 25 years, you get off of it. He said, that's too long. I had been on the church board 25 years. I said to my daughter, I said, well, I guess it's time I'm putting in my resignation. I said, I'll do that. When I came home, I had a young married man that I had worked with for about four years. He and I had worked together in the church. He was on the church board. I said, Cecil, you get off of that church board. I said, that minister says anybody that's been on the church board 25 years has been on there too long. I said, don't expect me to vote for you. I said, I'm getting off. I'm resigning. I said, I'm going to tell him right after that I don't want to be on the church board anymore. Cecil wouldn't do it. I didn't vote for him either, but he has been on the church board and now he's in his 70s. He's 10 years younger than I am. He's resigned now. They never would let him off of that church board. They had to have some elderly person on that church board.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about what it was like during the Depression for you and your family.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: It wasn't bad for us. Ray was out of work. I was living with my daughter at the time. We moved right in before, just as the Depression hit. They wanted us to come to Troy, my daughter and my son-in-law. They both wanted us to come down there. He went to work right in the post office. He got work. It wasn't very big wages at that time, but it was enough to pay our rent. Our board, we had lived good. We didn't have any trouble. I worked in the store for $15. I could buy enough groceries for near the last us a whole month at that time, that was during the Depression. We paid $15 a month rent. Then we had electric lights. We had fuel to buy. We burned wood stoves. We didn't save any money, but we got along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What store did you work in?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: At Green's. It was a big, dry, good store. It was a big, dry, good store. It was a big grocery store, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You must have been lucky to be able to get a job during the Depression at all.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I went to the post office to get a job. The post office man said, He said, I'll tell you, Mrs. Waldron, if I have to have help, I have to hire a man. I think his wife was a jealous woman. I've always thought that. He said, you tell your husband, if your husband will come down and put in his application, I'll hire him. He said, but I'm not hiring a woman. I sat right down and got the job. I got the job at the dry, good store and worked there. We didn't have any hard luck at all. My son-in-law was a washing water power man. He had studied a job all during the Depression. He came up a wealthy family. We didn't have any hardship. But John and Byers had hardship. John, especially, because he had a big family. I remember that Nagel had a man here then. His wife was kind of a snip. John went to ask for flour, for a sack of flour. I don't know if John ought to tell you about it, because I don't remember much about it. She acted up about giving him a sack of flour. She had no right to do that. John had eight little kids. If he needed an extra sack of flour, he should have had it. But he didn't get it. John was kind of timid, you know. But I've heard him tell about it. Those knothals were just kind of snippy. They weren't people that were capable that should have handled that. Because it was a lot of complaint among other people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About how the food...

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yeah, about how they'd treat them. Some of them had to want flour, or whatever it was that they wanted in the food line that they were entitled to have. John wasn't the only one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they in charge of distributing food for the company?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yeah, that's what they were doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He also controlled who would be working, too, didn't he? I think that at that time, Nagel did.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, it wasn't Nagel. It was his wife. I think that you'd find that you'd ask John about it, that when Mr. Nagel found it out, why? He got flour. It wasn't Nagel. It was his wife. She was kind of hungry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I had heard that there... I think it's true in most towns, but that there are some few people who like to feel that they're a little bit better than the others.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, I think that Nagel and his position would give her the big head. Because she was just a nothing hair and beauville until he got that job. She wasn't a woman that made friends and had much of a personality. She was just a big rube. That's really what she was. She was just a big fat rube. And she was a good woman, as far as I was concerned. I always liked her. That's what she was. She wasn't a very attractive person, ever. I think that it went to her head. I couldn't ever think of anything else. I was horrified when John told me about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, when I was talking with buyers, I happened to mention Frank Robinson to him and Psychiana, you know, that fella in Moscow that had been split in order. And he told me that Ray...

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, you know, Ray wasn't a bit religious. He was raised... His grandfather was a Methodist minister, and his father was a minister's son. And he was a religious man, Ray's father was. His mother was a very religious woman, but his mother died when he was only 12 years old. And he had an uncle that wanted him to become a minister. And Ray would stand up there. He'd have to go up there and stay over the weekend sometimes with his uncle. I don't know why. I don't remember. But he'd have to go up to this uncle. And this uncle wouldn't even let that kid throw a ball across the yard. He just had to sit. You'd beat your 12-year-old boy doing that on Sunday, Saturday and Sunday. And then his uncle would read the Bible. Well, you know, it just hardened Ray towards Christianity, something terrible. And when I first married him, why? I wasn't a Christian. I had been raised in a Christian home, but I had really never consecrated my life, you know, to the Lord. And really it was converted until I was quite old, 21 probably. And Ray, I thought he was an atheist. He acted to me just like an atheist. He didn't argue with me or anything like that. But he'd come out one time flatly and told me that if I ever became a Christian, he'd leave me. Well, I said, leave me, big boy. I'll just pack your suitcase right now and get out. Because I said, I'm going to spoke out with my sister and I said, I'm becoming a Christian. And I took it up to the church up there and told him just what he'd said. And they said, well, they said, you've done the right thing, Mrs. Waldron, if you have to make your choice. And he wants to leave you. Why? Let him, let him go. I came back and he never did leave me. He thought he'd buck me out, see. But he was finally converted. He was finally converted. Years went by and it was the funniest thing. You would just die laughing if you have time for me to tell it to you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, sure. Tell me.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I was in the, that's when we were living in Troy. That was during depression. And I still thought at that time the kids were grown and gone. We were alone. And I thought at that time that he was an atheist. I never argued or quarreled with him over religion. But he never tried to stop me going to Sunday school with the children or anything. He never, never, he was too fine a man to do a thing like that. And right down deep in his heart, he knew I was doing the right thing, don't you see? And I went to this big camp meeting and there was a Bible being sold there in the camp meeting, minister. And I wondered that Bible. Well, it was during the depression and the Bible was $20. I was big money at that time, you know. But I bought it. And I brought us home and Ray just looked at me. And he said, don't you know? He said, you shouldn't have bought that Bible now. He said, during the depression, he said, wait till the depression's over. And I said, well, I couldn't have gotten it then. And I said, it's right at my birthday. I said, you can call it my birthday present. And he kind of grinned at me. Well, my mother and dad came down to visit us. And Ray, they hadn't been in the house, I don't think, a half hour, sitting there talking, visiting. Ray says, I want to show you what I bought for my wife for her birthday. And he went in and got that big Bible brought back. Of course, my mother was delighted. My father thought it was nice, too. And I put near died laughing. I never said a word. I said, isn't it lovely? Isn't it nice? And I let go. But when I got my mother along, I told her, and she put near died laughing, too. And that was one funny instance. Well, one time our bathroom was right straight off from the kitchen, right at the bathroom door. And there was a cupboard right there. And there was a wood box. We burned woods, you know, right there. And I reached down to get a stick of wood, and Ray was in the bathroom, shaving. And I raised up and hit my head right on the corner of that cupboard. You know how badly that hurts. It put near, knocked me out. And I just stood there, you know, my head. And Ray stood there looking at me. He says, why don't you swear? And I said, well, what good would that do? And I said, you remember, I said, you're an atheist, and I'm a Christian. And I said, Christians don't swear. That's all I said. And he said, who said I was an atheist? Well, I said, you act like one. I said, I thought always you were an atheist. Well, I'm not an atheist. And I said, well, I'm glad to know that. And then I heard a program. There was a program going this the same morning. I was getting ready to go to church. And there was a program, and there was a minister that was preaching. And he was preaching a marvelous sermon. And I said, Ray, he was sitting there in the living room. I said, get the name of that minister for me, will you? I said, that's a wonderful sermon, and I'd like to know what his faith is. I said, I'd like to know, will you? And he said, yes. So I went ahead, dressing him. And he came out in the kitchen, and he said, his name is Soso. He said, he's a Methodist minister. He says, that's what I am. I'm a Methodist. And I says, well, how do you do? I said, I'm so happy to meet you. I said, I'm glad you're a Methodist. I just finished and died laughing. That was the funniest thing. The way he was that bad. He said, I'm a Methodist. And I said, well, how do you do? I'm just so happy to meet you, Mr. Methodist. His grandfather, see, was a Methodist minister. And this wasn't, was a Methodist minister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But during all these years, he didn't go to church.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I got him to go one time in Troy to a revival with me. And coming home, the streets were lighted, of course. And he saw different men coming out of some Lutheran church. I don't remember what church it was, but it was a church there in Troy. And Ray turned to me and he said that he knew the man, knew him well. He was coming down out there. He says, look at that old hypocrite going to church. And he says, he's the biggest old hypocrite in this country. And I said, I'm going to church, he said. He said, that's the reason I won't go to church. And I said, well, Ray, if you don't go to church and become a Christian, I said, you're going to hell. I said, do you know that? And the streets of hell will be just paved, I said, with hypocrites. I don't know what he thought about it. He never said a word. But I sure, we sure had some funny experiences over the years. And we didn't argue religion or anything like that, but he was converted. He finally became a Christian.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a particular experience that he had?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, no, I don't think so. I think it was just my life and living, seeing that it was the better life. He was an intelligent man, you know, and he realized as he grew older that, and his background, his father was a minister's son, a Christian man, and his mother was a fine Christian woman, and his grandfather, and I think that that all had its influence on him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But even though he wasn't active, he still was a very good person all the time.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: He was a good man all the time. He didn't drink or crouch or anything. He did smoke cigarettes, but he wasn't a tavern bum or anything like that. He was a good man, and his son is, and his grandson is the same way. His son, our son, never hangs around, never did hang, even when he was young, he never did hang around the taverns and drink beer and whiskey and tear around like that. I think maybe he did smoke, if I remember why, but he doesn't smoke now. And my grandson, my only grandson, never has smoked. He don't smoke, never did drink or crouch, and he never did hang around. He's just, it's their background. And then he's my only grandson, and of course he's always admired me like me, and he knows I'm a Christian. Well, he's a Christian, I thought.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the time that you went to Spokane, that time that you said you were going anyway, even though he didn't want you to go, you said you were going to become a Christian. Is that why you were going to Spokane?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yes, yes. There was an assembly up there, and my sister wanted to go, and my brother John, of course, would go, and we wanted to take in those meetings. They were having an assembly, and we wanted to take in those meetings. Well, my sister was an invalid, you know, and I wanted to go with her. And that was why I went to Spokane, was to be with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What denomination was that?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: The Church of God.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was Church of God, right?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Uh-huh, the Church of God. Not that cult. There's a cult that calls themselves the Church of God.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, you told me when I was here before about that.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: But that's not the same. No, it's not that cult. Well, I had mentioned Frank Robinson to you, and- That was before Ray was ever converted. That was an experience. And this great- He was a big man, Psychiana Robinson, a great big man. And Ray run the post office because the man that was ahead of Ray in the office was always gone. And Ray just took care of that post office, and he didn't make any difference to him. So that other thought was just to take off. Well, anyway, he was alone in there that day. And this great big, fine-looking man came in and he bought $500 worth of stamps all at once. And Ray said he never heard such cursing in all his life. He was mad at the post office in Moscow, and he was going to make a first-class post office out of Troy. He was going to do all his business in Troy. And Ray just was horrified. He said, I've lived with the Lumberjacks and I never heard such cursing. He says, come home, and he said to me, well, he says, you know, Kate, he says, I met the devil incarnate today. And I said, what? And he says, I met the devil incarnate today. He says, if I ever saw the devil, I saw him today. And then he told me, he said that Psychiana Robinson from Moscow came in and bought $500 worth of stamps at one time. And he said he was so mad at the Moscow post office that he said, I never heard such cursing in all my life. He said, I have never seen anything like that. He says, I tell you, I saw the devil incarnate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he say, did Robinson say why he was mad at the Moscow post office?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No, he didn't explain a thing to Ray. But he was mad at him. And he was going to bring every bit of his business. And he was, it would be a lot, it was, oh, he had an enormous business, you know, because he had people from all over the world. He preyed on rich widows. That's who he preyed on. And he'd buy as high as $500 to $1,000 worth of stamps at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he come back and use the tri-post office after that?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No, he never did come back. I don't know whether they straightened things out in Moscow or gave him his way, whatever he wanted. He got. And because he never came back. And Ray didn't want him to. He said, I don't ever want to meet that man. Now, he horrified Ray just that badly. And it must have been terrible. Because Ray wasn't a man that was religious himself in any way, you know, and to be so horrified that he really thought that. But he said, I never saw anybody, he said, like that man. He said he was the worst cursing I ever heard in all my life. And he had been among the lumberjacks for years, and they were bad to swear. But he sure. And of course, Psyche Anna didn't live too many years after that. He died. But his wife never, never did accept his religion. She was a fine Christian woman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know her at all?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No, but people did know her that told me about her. She said she was a lovely woman and that she didn't approve of him and his business at all. She knew he wasn't right, that he shouldn't do that. But she stayed with him until he died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She went to the Presbyterian Church in Moscow, I understand, regularly.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yeah. Yeah. He was a peculiar character. Very peculiar. I guess he had a regular following there in Moscow. I never did find out. I never did ask. But he must have had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: My understanding is that he did almost all of it by mail, and that he tried, he refused to sell his literature to people in Moscow because I think he was afraid or didn't want to get the other denominations going against him. Because they weren't very happy about what he was doing anyway. And if he was trying to convert people in Moscow, it would have aroused a lot of anger, I think, towards him. So I don't think he really had a big following in Moscow, but he had all over the country.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yeah, all over the world. But I don't believe he did either because I never did meet anybody that approved of him in Moscow. They all even talk of him today. They say, my, he was an awful character. He was just an awful character. Of course, that didn't reflect race about him, so I never did see him annoying. I didn't have any desire to meet him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was it that you, when he moved to Moscow, and then you started to work with him, Franklin?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, it was during the Depression. During the Depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was curious about, you told me that you were the head clerk at the store.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, yes, at the Ben Franklin store for years. I was head lady.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that work like at that time?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: It was just clerking. I worked all over the store. And it was, we had dry goods and a hardware counter and stationary and just everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much responsibility?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, yes. I had a terrible lot of responsibility. I don't see how I ever kept up under it. I had to train in every girl that came in the store. I had to okay every check, and I had to recount every bill the girls would put down their sales, you know, and add them. And then they'd have to bring that slip to me, and I had to re-add it. And I didn't have an adding machine to work on. And I was a change girl. I handled all the change. And I had to take care of the girls. They had 15 minutes off in the morning for coffee. Well, they had to come to me and get permission to go. They were the ones that was leaving. So I know when they should be back in 15 minutes, you know. I had an awful lot of, and I did buying.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did the buying?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I did buying. I had quite a lot of experience doing buying with those salesmen. I had one big fella come in. He was just a swearing like a blue streak. And he was going on and on. And I said, now listen here, little boy. I said, if you want me to look at your merchandise, I said, you just pipe down. I said, I don't swear. And I don't like swearing. And I said, if you can't show me your sock without swearing, I said, just close your suitcase and get out. And the manager was right up in the office, and he could hear every word I said. And he approved of it just highly. And you know, that man apologized. And I never, never did he ever swear again. That was one experience I had. And those salesmen are dirty minded, the majority of them. They're just full, you know, of dirty stories. And they never did tell me a dirty story. I said, just go and pedal that stuff. I said, did the ones that like it? I said, I don't like them. I said, I don't tell dirty stories. And I said, I don't listen to them. Well, now, Kate, this is just a good story. This is just a cute story. I said, go and tell it to somebody else. I said, I don't trust you. And we had a superintendent ever so often, we'd have a superintendent come in. And he came in, and we were working for Jim David then. And you know, Jim was only about 27. And he didn't want us clerks calling him Mr. David. He gave us orders to call him Jim. And of course, I was old enough to be his mother. And he didn't want me calling him Mr. David. He said, I want you all to call me Jim. So we called him Jim. Well, the superintendent came in. And those superintendents kind of watched the star in every way. And they changed things. And they talked to the clerks and tell them how they should do and what they should do. Well, he called us together under Jim. And he said, he bought us out. Oh, he bought some of the girls out. He didn't say anything directly to me. But he was showing the girls some of the things that they did that I let them get by with. It didn't bother me. It bothered him. And he was a swear on the way, you know, when he stopped. And it went all, got all through. And then I said to him, I said, are you through? He said, yes. I said, now I'm going to tell you a few things. All right, Kate. He said, just what do you want? I said, I don't like your swearing. I wish you could have seen me. He'd better swear on the blue streak. I said, I don't like the way you swear. And he didn't say anything for a few minutes. And then he said, well, Kate. He said, don't you ever, ever say darn or damn? And my manager, Jim, said, she never says anything like that. Never. And he said, well, you know, Kate, he said, I quit drinking and I quit smoking. And he said, I have tried to quit swearing, but he says, you know, Kate, I can't. Well, I said, I still don't like it. And I looked at him and smiled. I said, I just don't like it. And I said, you could quit if you wanted to. I said, that's just a dirty habit, lack of vocabulary. I said, that's what's the matter with your lack of vocabulary. You can't express yourself without swearing. Well, the next morning, he come over to me and he says, Kate, come over here. I want to show you something. He took me over at a girl's counter way over on the other side of the store. He said, look at that counter. You know, Jim was just a playboy. That store went broke under Jim and I knew it would when he took it over. I knew that it would because why? He was nothing but a playboy. Mr. David had no business putting him in there at all. And he said, look at that counter. And I looked at it and I said, yes, I said, it looks bad. He says, it looks just like the devil, don't it? Oh, he says, I beg your pardon, Kate. He said, I didn't mean to say that. I couldn't help but laugh, you know. I said, oh, that's all right. I said, it really does. But it was funny. I just got such a kick out of that fellow. He was a nice guy. I learned to like him awfully well, but he was pretty careful when he was around me with his swearing. And I had another experience with the superintendent. I was a good saleswoman. That's why they kept raising my wages all the time because I really loved to sell. And I was a good salesman. If I do say it myself, I didn't have any trouble selling things. David's had bring down everything they couldn't sell up there in their own store and I'd sell it. I'd sell it for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I didn't know that David's owned, that David's actually owned this.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yeah, they bought this store when Jim was working there and put Jim in there. That was his son, see. And he put Jim in there and Jim was only 27 years old and he was just a perfect playboy. And I was going to tell you personally, he wasn't too bright. Jim wasn't too bright. He was one of the nicest people I have ever known. He wouldn't say an unkind word to you to hurt your feelings if his life depended on it because his father just browbeated him to death. I learned to just love Jim and I felt so sorry for him because he had heart trouble. I just kiddied him. My heart just went out to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which one was Jim's father? Was that Homer?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: That was Homer. Homer was his father.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he kind of dominated.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yes, and he abused Jim time. I just saw that him browbeated that boy to something terrible. Just oh, I just loved him. I just my heart just ache burned because I knew that he wasn't real bright. I don't know whether old David knew that or not. I just don't know, but he wasn't. I knew it. I could tell it, but the things that he do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say he was a playboy, he really wasn't serious about the business.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No, he wasn't. He just didn't care. I went down the basement one time and I brought up 27 articles that should have been upstairs that that girl should have had on her counter. Now that was his business. That wasn't my business. My business was to sell it. And it had to be there to sell. And I said, Jim, when he came back from lunch, I said, come over here. I want to show you something. And I took him over. I said, I have brought up 27 articles, Jim, that should be on that counter. Where in the world did you find him, Kate? I said, down the basement. I said, they're down the basement there. They've been shipped in and they're there and marked. But I said, the girls don't go down there and get them. And I tried to help him every way that I could because I thought the world, Jim. I just thought the world. And this superintendent come, and when they'd come, they'd just work him to death. And this superintendent, it seemed to me that he was just overstepping his authority. And I said to him, I said, do you know that Jim has heart trouble awfully, Dad? And he said, no, I didn't know that. Well, I said, you'd just better lay off of that kid. I said, and quit working him so hard because I said, if you don't, he's going to have a heart attack. Now, I said, I've been with him for several years here. And I said, Jim can't work like that. And I said, you're just working him to death. And he said, well, he said, I will. He said, I won't do any more, Kate. He said, I'm so glad you told me. And he left the next morning. He told Jim, he said, I have to stay here as long as I can stay. But he was just working Jim to death. He didn't know that Jim had heart trouble. And Jim was a great big, you know, massive man. You wouldn't think of him having a heart condition, but he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And these supervisors, where did they come from?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I've seen him when he'd worked there in the store. And he'd have one of those spells. And he'd be just as white as that lampshade. And I'd say, now, Jim, you just better be quiet. You just better sit down and be quiet. Don't be trying to do anything more. And I knew his condition. But of course, these superintendents had come in there. They didn't know his condition. And it depended on a lot where he'd been the night before. He'd been up gambling and tearing around. Why, you see that next day, working him to death. Why, it was just terrible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These superintendents, where did they come from?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, they were head men. You see, that was called the Ben Franklin store. And there are big Ben Franklin stores over the country now, too. And they have leaders that come in that kind of oversee everything. Well, Mr. David, the old man, Homer, Jim's father, put Jim in there. And he just kept these superintendents coming and paid them. And he knew they'd be a help to Jim, which they would, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the store was really owned by David's.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yeah, it was owned by David.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it was still part of that Ben Franklin. Yes.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: He would hire these men, pay them extra to come every month. They'd come every month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the store go down?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Yeah, the store went down, went clear down. And they sold it to Justice. I worked for Mr. Justice for years. I would have worked at Creighton's. They wanted me up there. But Mr. Justice just raped my wages.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This Mr. Justice, that's not shorty.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No, no, that's another Justice. No, they'd build him a beautiful home there in Moscow. They made a fortune. They kept the Ben Franklin store. They bought it from David. And then they bought into the other Ten Cent store. And they run the Plunk store for years. And they made a fortune there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what happened to Jim?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, Jim and his wife separated. And they went down to California. Jim died. He didn't live to be very old. He didn't live many years after I worked with him. He worked for a while in the David store with his father up there. Jim was a good clerk. He was a good salesman. And I went in there time and again. And Jim had always said, Come on, come on up here and work with us. And I'd go in there and they'd have a sale. And the clerk would say, Come on and go take off your coat and come and help us. I just hated to go in there. And even Homer was after me trying to get in. Virginia, my niece, Mrs. Chet Morris, this is me one time, says, We don't want her working anymore. Do you understand that? And Homer said to her, he said, Well, you come and put it for me then. And Virginia said, I don't want to work. She said, I don't want to work. And she says, I don't want Andy Cape working anymore either. But I worked till I was tired of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me something about the selling. You say you really enjoyed selling. What was it about it that you liked?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I don't know. It's just fascinating. I couldn't tell you what it was about it. I was a good salesman. I could sell anything I'd start off to sell. And I remember one Christmas, I heard Bill Thompson run the David store before Jim took over. And it was when Ben Franklin, when David bought it and put Mr. Thompson in there as manager, Mr. Thompson and I together built that store up. Because Mr. Thompson, whenever some of the superintendents had come, when I first went to work there, when I first went to work, why Mr. Thompson would take me up in the office and introduce me to these men, and he'd said, She is my salesman, my helper. He, she and I are building this store up. And when he left there and went over and took over the Potlatch store, he tried his best to get me to come over there. But I didn't want to go to Potlatch. And we built the store up, Mr. Thompson and I. Mr. Thompson was a good manager, a good head man. And then I was a good saleswoman. And we built the store up. Well, then Mr. Thompson got a better job over Potlatch. He left. And they put Jim in there. And Thompson said to me, when he was trying to get me to come out and move over, he said, Why can't you move over there? It's this race working in Bogle, home over the weekend. He said, He'd be just, it wouldn't be any harder for him to get home. And I said, Oh, I don't want to. I don't want to go over there. I don't, I've been in Potlatch. I said, I don't want to leave Moscow. The church was in Moscow, you know. And I said, I want to be where the church is. And he said, You know, okay, it's going to be rough here. Because he said, That kid will never put this store over the top. He said, He'll go rope just as sure as the world. And I said, Well, I said, Maybe he will. Maybe he won't. I said, I hope he won't. Well, he said, He will. You wait and see. He said, You wait and see. I give him two years. And he said, This store will go broke. Now Mr. Thompson told me that. And so when Mr. Jim's father came down, he said, Now Jim, he said, Mrs. Waldron has been here with Bill, and they built this store up. He said, Now you work. Mrs. Waldron will help you, and you work together. Well, that was the worst thing he could have said to Jim. Jim was a young fella, 27 years old, just a boy. And his dad's saying that and browbeating him down all the time like it always had. Jim wasn't going to let some old woman come in there and boss him. Can't you see it? I could see it. I knew it just as well. But Jim learned to like me, and I liked Jim. We were the greatest of friends, and I helped him all that I could. But I couldn't go to his father and tell him. I knew the store was going broke. After six months, I knew that it was just a question at a time until the store broke. Because I knew why it was just rotten the way that store was running. It was just plain rotten. Just plain rotten.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that he was doing wrong? He just wasn't staying up following what was going on?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: The girls just neglected their counters, and they'd sit down and have books and read them instead of being up working on their counters. I was really the only one that paid attention to business at all. Jim, sometimes he'd come and look at my till, and he'd just shake his head. He'd say, I don't see how you do it. He'd go to the other till, the girls' other till, and they didn't have hardly anything in them. They were just laying off on the job. Don't you see that? Sometimes it'd be three or four of them up in the office talking and visiting, cutting up with him. It was just a regular playhouse there. I was just burned up two or three times. I thought I'd quit. But I thought, oh, as long as I'm doing my part, not laying down on the job, I felt sorry for Jim because his dad had come down there and just bawling out something terrible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He'd come down every now and then and be angry about it.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: And be angry with Jim. Jim would never say a word. My heart would just go out to him and pity him. I just pitied him. I just couldn't leave him. I stayed with him to the end. Because they paid my wage, it's just the same. I kept up my work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In front of you?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: No, he didn't bawl him out in front of any of the other girls. But he didn't care if I was around. But he wouldn't bawl him out around the other girls. But if he did, the other girls would have been mad at him. They would have stood right by Jim because they all liked Jim. Jim was the loveliest person. He wouldn't say an unkind word to hurt anybody's feelings. If it's life, you can't do that. He was gentle as a lamb. And he just wasn't fit for a manager because the clerks, some of them you just have to talk to, you know. It's a manager's place to do it. Believe me, Bill Thompson did it. He said, if you're going to work here, you get behind that counter and you stay behind it and you keep on the job and you keep your counter up and you get out. That's what he did with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think it was? You told me, you say you're not sure what it is that you like so much about selling, but what do you think it was that made you a good saleswoman?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, I think that I was very nice to the people. I was friendly. I met a woman over here at this party yesterday and I didn't know her from Adams Hall of Foc. And she come up to me and took me and she says, Kate, she said, I know you. I knew you when you clerked in Moscow and you waited on me many times. And I had had that happen to me hundreds of times. I go to Moscow and I'll be maybe in safe way shopping. Some woman will come up and say, well, how are you, Mrs. Walden? I haven't seen you in years. And I was so glad and so sorry when you quit working. I've had hundreds of problems. And I guess it was because I like people. I love people. I love to be with people. And I was nice to them. And if there was some old lady come in that needed special attention, I gave it to her. And I heard one lady say to another one time, said, if Mrs. Walden tells you that goods won't fade, it won't fade. Said, you can depend on anything she tells you. And I guess that's why I like this. I like clerking very much. And I like selling. I used to sell when I was a little tiny girl. Mm-hmm. You know how kids do. They see an ad. And I remember I sold Cloverine South. And I said, didn't get that salve today. It's the finest salve I think on the market today. And I used to sell a little box about like that for 25 cents. And then there was a pretty picture, though, with it. And I used to make spending money. And one time I sold silk hose. Not silk hose, but good hose. They didn't have silk hose. They had just good cotton hose. And mom would let me. She'd say, well, if you want to try it, why send and get it. She'd give me the money, the first money you have to pay, you know. And I'd go over to town. I'd make pretty good spending money for myself. I just naturally like to sell. And I think I learned a lot, too, in that. Because I enjoyed my work very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were at Ben Franklin's, what did you sell mostly? Was it women's clothing and that sort of thing?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, yes. We had children's clothing and hose, silk hose, and all kind of baby clothes. I had a big counter of baby clothes. I had three big counters. And I loved to decorate. I think my real colleague was an interior decorator. Because I just loved. And one time, Mr. Justice said to me, she says, you know, OK, I wish we could pay you to just decorate. And not let you do anything else but just decorate. Because I would decorate the store, my walls back of me, you know. And I just love to do work like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I can see it in your house and on your grounds now. You have that flair. Yes.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: I think we've talked about it. My daughter and my niece since we've gotten older. And every one of them thinks that my real calling is an interior decorator.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do you think that that started to show itself?

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Well, I think it did. I think it commenced showing in me after I was married and had my own home. Have you ever seen my kitchen? No. Well, my house is not swell. You know that or great. But come see my kitchen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it's pretty. Yeah.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: And my...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With... of faith healing.

speaker: Kate Sanderson Waldron
words: Oh, yes. Yes, I have. I believe definitely in divine healing. I've had several wonderful healings. I got up one morning and I wake up in the night and I couldn't turn over at all. I was in such pain. I didn't know what in the world was the matter with me. But I just couldn't move. I couldn't turn over. Well, in the morning I tried to get out of bed and I just... I couldn't... I wanted to unlock that door. So when John and Byers came, they could come in. If I couldn't help myself that they could get in, you know. They'd come and the door was locked. They'd think I had gone somewhere because they don't come to about 9, 10 o'clock. And I couldn't get out of bed. And I just said, Lord, I said, I just can't do this. I said, you have to help me. I said, I... When I'm praying to the same... Jesus walked the shores of Galilee and healed the people. And I said, I know you can heal me. And I laid there a few minutes and raised up and got out of bed. And every pain was gone. There wasn't a thing in the world wrong with me. And I unlocked the door. And I did my work. And there wasn't a thing wrong with me. I was just healed completely. I felt like a... I'd never been able to express to tell you how I felt. I felt just as light as a feather. And I felt just as good as I felt when I was a girl. I was just touched completely and healed. And that was one experience I had. And not long ago, I had an experience. I was getting breakfast. And I was taken sick. And I think... I know when I'm going to faint, I can tell. I get deathly sick. And the blood just drains out of my face. I get just as white as snow. And if I can just lay down, I lay down just as quick as I can so I won't fall. And I don't know how long I lay in the faint. I haven't any idea I'm alone. And I come out of it finally. And I'm all right. And anyway, I was sick. And I was afraid of fainting. I didn't faint, but I was afraid of me. And I got up and felt that terrible, nauseated feeling. And I got out. And I couldn't hardly get my breakfast. I put the coffee pot on. It made me some toast. Put me a couple pieces of bacon. And I sat down to the table. I couldn't hardly get down to the table. I just was just sick. I just was flunky. And I turned thanks, of course, as I always do. And I prayed for myself. I said, I don't know what this is, Lord.
